chunks.title: Catalog Description
Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
This is an introductory course designed for any student interested in using computation to enhance
their problem-solving abilities. No prior experience in programming is necessary. Students will
use their problem-solving abilities to implement programs in Python.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Develop Computational Thinking: The learner can apply concepts in computation to
enhance problemp-solving abilities across various disciplines.
• Implement Python Programs: The learner can design, write, and execute functional
programs in the Python programming language.
• Understand Data Representation: The learner can comprehend how data is internally
represented within a computer and how people communicate with computing systems.
• Degug and Analyze Behavior: The learner can identify and fix errors while analyzing the
behavior and limitations of specific programs.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Programming in Python 3
ISBN: 978-1-394-02737-8
Authors: Bailey Miller
Publisher: zyBooks
Notes:
How to purchase the textbook:
• Sign in or create an account at learn.zybooks.com
• Enter zyBooks code: TAMUCSCE110YumSpring2026
• Subscribe (A subscription fee is $69. Students may begin subscribing on
December 29, 2025. Subscriptions will last until May 25, 2026.)

chunks.title: Grading Table
chunks.content:
| Course Grading |
|---|
| 1 Midterm | 30% |
| 9 Programming Assignments | 10% |
| Final exam | 30% |
| 5 Homeworks | 10% |
| 5 Quizzes | 20% |

*** No extra credit/extra work will be given at the end of the semester. ***

chunks.title: Final Grades:
chunks.content:
Final grades will be assigned as follows:
90-100 A
80-89 B
70-79 C
60-69 D
Below 60 F

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Programming assignments and homeworks should be submitted on or before the due dates.
Programming assignment and homework grades will lose 25% for each day after the due dates.

chunks.title: List of Topics:
chunks.content:
• Introduction to Python 1 week
• Variables and Expressions 2 weeks
• Types, Branches, and Loops 1 week
• Functions, Recursion and Strings 3 weeks
• Lists, Tuples, Sets, and Dictionaries 2 weeks
• Classes 2 weeks
• Exceptions 1 week
• Modules and Files 1 week
• Inheritance 1 week

chunks.title: Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
Assignments will require use of standard programming style conventions and
documentation which will be illustrated in lab and lecture examples.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct,
plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or
words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit
your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation
styles. Artificial Intelligence (Al) text generators and natural language processing
tools (colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code, video, and
image generators should not be used for any work for this class without explicit
permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not
limited to,
i. Creating or revising drafts
ii. Editing your work
iii. Reviewing a peer's work
This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar
checkers, which are acceptable.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
Attendance at all lectures is required to ensure that each student understands the informational
content of the class and becomes aware of any changes to the tentative class exam, quiz, and
assignment schedules as noted later in this document. Absence from a lecture is not a valid excuse
to be misinformed about any class activity, and the student maintains the responsibility for all
course content.
